growth_records.medium: defined minimal medium (full salt composition in SI Appendix; not in main text)
growth_records.cite: Mendonca et al. Hierarchical routing in carbon metabolism favors iron-scavenging strategy in iron-deficient soil Pseudomonas species. Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A (2020). DOI:10.1073/pnas.2016380117. [PMC7768705]
growth_records.snippet: there were near-equal consumption rates of both substrates in the Fe-replete cells (19.8 ± 4.4 mmol C gCDW−1 h−1 and 27.2 ± 8.1 mmol C gCDW−1 h−1 for glucose and benzoate, respectively)
growth_records.notes: P. putida KT2440 on 1:1 carbon-equivalent glucose:benzoate, Fe-replete [(+)Fe]. Reported uptake: glucose 19.8±4.4, benzoate 27.2±8.1 mmol C/gCDW/h. Total carbon influx 47.0±9.2 mM C/gCDW/h (matches 19.8+27.2=47.0). CO2 efflux estimated 19.6±0.98 mM C/gCDW/h. Rates are per carbon-mole (mmol C), not per compound-mole. Base medium salt composition and T/pH are in SI Appendix, not in the fetched XML. Total carbon-equivalent 100 or 300 mM as stated in main text; mixed substrate = equimolar carbon 1:1. No PVD/gluconate rate reported for (+)Fe. Growth rate used in Fig 7 not given numerically in text.

growth_records.medium: defined minimal medium, intermediate Fe-limited [Int(-)Fe] (full salt composition in SI Appendix)
growth_records.cite: Mendonca et al. Hierarchical routing in carbon metabolism favors iron-scavenging strategy in iron-deficient soil Pseudomonas species. Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A (2020). DOI:10.1073/pnas.2016380117. [PMC7768705]
growth_records.snippet: the carbon influx rate ... was 56% higher in the intermediate Fe-limited condition (73.5 ± 11 mM C gCDW−1 h−1) ... PVD secretion rate under the intermediate Fe-limited condition (41.4 ± 3.59 µmol gCDW−1 h−1)
growth_records.notes: P. putida KT2440 on 1:1 glucose:benzoate, intermediate Fe-limited. Total carbon influx 73.5±11 mM C/gCDW/h (stated 56% higher than Fe-replete 47.0). Benzoate consumed 57% faster than glucose (no absolute per-substrate values). PVD secretion 41.4±3.59 umol/gCDW/h. CO2 efflux 39.1±2.1 mM C/gCDW/h (2-fold higher than 19.6 replete). Siderophore = 3.5% of carbon influx. Growth rate not given numerically.

growth_records.medium: defined minimal medium, severe Fe-limited [(-)Fe] phase 1 / benzoate-consumption phase (full salt composition in SI Appendix)

up.method: reported (measured depletion; rapid phase-1 benzoate consumption)

sec.method: reported (SI Appendix Fig S4A; benzoate-consumption phase)
estimated (carbon balance, Fig 7A; 20% higher than 19.6 replete)

growth_records.cite: Mendonca et al. Hierarchical routing in carbon metabolism favors iron-scavenging strategy in iron-deficient soil Pseudomonas species. Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A (2020). DOI:10.1073/pnas.2016380117. [PMC7768705]
growth_records.snippet: benzoate depletion (64.5 ± 1.2 mmol C gCDW−1 h−1) occurred rapidly ... near 2-fold increase in the PVD secretion rate (91.8 ± 15.2 µmol gCDW−1 h−1) during the first phase ... carbon influx ... 41% higher in first phase (66.1 ± 1.4 mM C gCDW
growth_records.notes: P. putida KT2440 on 1:1 glucose:benzoate, severe Fe-limited PHASE 1 (benzoate primary). Benzoate uptake 64.5±1.2 mmol C/gCDW/h; total carbon influx 66.1±1.4 mM C/gCDW/h. PVD secretion 91.8±15.2 umol/gCDW/h. Gluconate secretion 11.6±4.6 umol/gCDW/h (benzoate phase). CO2 efflux 23.4±1.2 mM C/gCDW/h. Siderophore = 8.6% of carbon influx. Rates per carbon-mole. Growth rate not given numerically.

growth_records.medium: defined minimal medium, severe Fe-limited [(-)Fe] phase 2 / glucose-consumption phase (full salt composition in SI Appendix)

up.method: reported (measured depletion; also = total carbon influx in phase 2)

sec.method: reported (SI Appendix Fig S4A; 200-fold higher than benzoate-phase 11.6 umol/gCDW/h)
estimated (carbon balance, Fig 7A; 73% lower than 19.6 replete)

growth_records.cite: Mendonca et al. Hierarchical routing in carbon metabolism favors iron-scavenging strategy in iron-deficient soil Pseudomonas species. Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A (2020). DOI:10.1073/pnas.2016380117. [PMC7768705]
growth_records.snippet: glucose depletion (15.2 ± 0.2 mmol C gCDW−1 h−1) ... 5-fold decrease in the PVD secretion rate (17.5 ± 2.86 µmol gCDW−1 h−1) ... 200-fold increase in gluconate secretion (from 11.6 ± 4.6 µmol gCDW−1 h−1 to 2.3 ± 0.3 mmol gCDW−1 h−1)
growth_records.notes: P. putida KT2440 on 1:1 glucose:benzoate, severe Fe-limited PHASE 2 (glucose only, benzoate exhausted). Glucose uptake 15.2±0.2 mmol C/gCDW/h (= total carbon influx, 3-fold lower than replete 47.0). PVD 17.5±2.86 umol/gCDW/h. Gluconate 2.3±0.3 mmol/gCDW/h (200-fold up vs phase-1 11.6 umol/gCDW/h; 52% of carbon efflux). CO2 efflux 5.24±0.3 mM C/gCDW/h. Note carbon-balance discrepancy: total efflux 26.7±2.7 > influx 15.2 (attributed to carbon retained from phase 1). Growth rate not given numerically.

growth_records.medium: defined minimal medium, Fe-limited, glucose as sole carbon source (full salt composition in SI Appendix)
growth_records.cite: Mendonca et al. Hierarchical routing in carbon metabolism favors iron-scavenging strategy in iron-deficient soil Pseudomonas species. Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A (2020). DOI:10.1073/pnas.2016380117. [PMC7768705]
growth_records.snippet: Fe-limited cells grown on glucose alone had a similar PVD secretion rate (13.0 ± 8.0 µmol gCDW−1 h−1)
growth_records.notes: P. putida KT2440, Fe-limited, glucose as the SOLE carbon source (distinct from the mixed glucose:benzoate conditions). Reported PVD (pyoverdine siderophore) secretion 13.0±8.0 umol/gCDW/h, described as similar to the glucose-only phase-2 rate. No glucose uptake, CO2, or growth rate numerically reported for this single-substrate condition in the main text. Confidence medium (only the one secretion rate is quantified).

up.method: reported qS in Fig 1c (specific glucose uptake rate determined by linear regression of log-transformed OD600 during exponential growth); numeric value only in figure image, not in text

sec.method: reported molar yield of gluconate on glucose (max extracellular concn during exponential growth)
reported molar yield of 2-ketogluconate on glucose

yields.Yxs: reported in Fig 1c (numeric value only in figure image, not in text)

growth_records.cite: Nikel et al. Reconfiguration of metabolic fluxes in Pseudomonas putida as a response to sub-lethal oxidative stress. The ISME Journal (2021). DOI:10.1038/s41396-020-00884-9. [PMC8163872]
growth_records.snippet: the corresponding molar yields of gluconate and 2-ketogluconate on glucose in control experiments were yG/S = 0.31 ± 0.09 and yK/S = 0.12 ± 0.02 C-mol C-mol−1, respectively.
growth_records.notes: Control (no H2O2) glucose batch culture of P. putida KT2440. Text explicitly states secretion product molar yields on glucose: gluconate yG/S = 0.31 ± 0.09 C-mol C-mol^-1; 2-ketogluconate yK/S = 0.12 ± 0.02 C-mol C-mol^-1 (periplasmic sugar-oxidation products, later re-consumed). μ, qS and Yx/s were determined (regression of log-transformed OD600 during exponential growth; Yx/s at 24 h after glucose exhaustion) but their numeric values are only in Fig 1c image, not in the text, so left null (not invented). Culture temperature not stated in Methods (only extraction/drying temps given), so temperature_C null. Reported catabolic NADPH overproduction (intracellular, not a secretion) R_NADPH surplus = 1.3 mmol gCDW^-1 h^-1 under control — noted for context only.

up.method: reported qS in Fig 1c; stated to be essentially identical to control (glucose uptake rate unchanged by H2O2); numeric value only in figure image

sec.method: reported molar yield of gluconate on glucose under 1.5 mM H2O2
reported molar yield of 2-ketogluconate on glucose under 1.5 mM H2O2

yields.Yxs: reported in Fig 1c, essentially same as control (numeric value only in figure)

growth_records.cite: Nikel et al. Reconfiguration of metabolic fluxes in Pseudomonas putida as a response to sub-lethal oxidative stress. The ISME Journal (2021). DOI:10.1038/s41396-020-00884-9. [PMC8163872]
growth_records.snippet: In the presence of oxidative stress, yG/S and yK/S were 0.19 ± 0.08 and 0.05 ± 0.01 C-mol C-mol−1 ... the flux of NADPH generation increased to 14.4 ± 0.3 mmol gCDW−1 h−1 upon exposure of the cells to H2O2
growth_records.notes: Sub-lethally H2O2-stressed (1.5 mM) glucose batch culture. Secretion product molar yields (halved vs control, reflecting reduced periplasmic glucose oxidation): gluconate yG/S = 0.19 ± 0.08; 2-ketogluconate yK/S = 0.05 ± 0.01 C-mol C-mol^-1. μ, qS and Yx/s were reported not significantly different from control but numeric values are only in Fig 1c image (left null, not invented). Intracellular NADPH generation flux increased to 14.4 ± 0.3 mmol gCDW^-1 h^-1 (~50% surplus over biosynthetic demand) under stress — intracellular flux, not a secretion, noted for context. Culture temperature not stated in Methods.

up.units: sole carbon source (15 mM); specific uptake rate not reported

growth_records.cite: de Witt et al. Upcycling of polyamides through chemical hydrolysis and engineered Pseudomonas putida. Nature Microbiology (2025). DOI:10.1038/s41564-025-01929-5. [PMC11879879]
growth_records.snippet: indicating the emergence of stable mutations that enabled growth at a rate of ~0.13 +/- 0.01 h-1 (Supplementary Table 1).
growth_records.notes: Reported mu = 0.13 +/- 0.01 h-1 for the HMDA-1 mutant on HMDA as sole carbon source (Growth Profiler OD-based). doubling_time = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.13 = 5.33 h. MSM full recipe is by reference (ref 57); only 4-fold phosphate buffer (K2HPO4 15.52 g/L, NaH2PO4 6.52 g/L) and carbon source (15 mM) are explicitly stated, so medium_composition_stated=false.

growth_records.medium: Mineral salts medium (MSM), 4-fold buffered, with C6-polyamide monomer (HMDA, 6-aminohexanoic acid [Ahx], or epsilon-caprolactam) as sole carbon source

up.units: sole carbon source (15 mM each); specific uptake not reported

growth_records.cite: de Witt et al. Upcycling of polyamides through chemical hydrolysis and engineered Pseudomonas putida. Nature Microbiology (2025). DOI:10.1038/s41564-025-01929-5. [PMC11879879]
growth_records.snippet: the resulting strain (PP_2884-Delta3 PP_0409-W676L, designated as P. putida NYL) showed identical growth compared to the ALE mutant Ahx-194 with HMDA, Ahx and epsilon-caprolactam as sole carbon source, with estimated growth rates between 0.
growth_records.notes: P. putida NYL grows on all three C6-PA monomers at estimated mu = 0.07-0.108 h-1. Grew better on Ahx than HMDA-1 strain. Kept growth_rate null because value is a substrate-spanning range, not a single explicit number. MSM full recipe by reference (ref 57); only buffer + carbon source stated -> medium_composition_stated=false.

growth_records.snippet: Using Ahx as substrate, 7.7 +/- 0.2% (g PHB per g CDW) was produced.
growth_records.notes: PHB product yield = 7.7 +/- 0.2 % (g PHB / g CDW) for P. putida NYLON-PHB on 30 mM Ahx as sole C and N source. Value explicitly reported; PHB quantified by acidic methanolysis + GC vs 3-hydroxybutanoic acid standard. Growth rate for this production run not given in accessible text.

growth_records.snippet: For HMDA and epsilon-caprolactam, 13.2 +/- 1.0% and 8.2 +/- 0.3% (g PHB per g CDW) were produced, respectively.

growth_records.medium: MSM supplemented with PA6 hydrolysate (Ahx + linear Ahx2-7 oligomers), diluted to be C-mol equivalent to 30 mM Ahx

up.compound: Ahx and linear Ahx oligomers (Ahx2-7) from PA6 hydrolysate
up.units: all soluble linear oligomers consumed within 48 h; Ahx depleted first then shorter then longer oligomers

product_yields.PHB: 7.0 +/- 0.3 % g PHB/g CDW on PA6 hydrolysate (not significantly different from pure Ahx)

growth_records.cite: de Witt et al. Upcycling of polyamides through chemical hydrolysis and engineered Pseudomonas putida. Nature Microbiology (2025). DOI:10.1038/s41564-025-01929-5. [PMC11879879]
growth_records.snippet: all oligomers were metabolized by P. putida NYLON-PHB within 48 h, resulting in growth to a final optical density at 600 nm (OD600) of 5.75 ... PHB contents in the cells reached 7.0 +/- 0.3% (g PHB per g CDW).
growth_records.notes: PHB yield = 7.0 +/- 0.3 % (g PHB/g CDW) growing on PA6 acid hydrolysate (Ahx + linear Ahx2-7) at 30 mM Ahx C-mol equivalent; final OD600 = 5.75, all soluble linear oligomers consumed in 48 h. Values explicitly reported.

growth_records.cite: Yang et al. Comparing in planta accumulation with microbial routes to set targets for a cost-competitive bioeconomy. Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A (2022). DOI:10.1073/pnas.2122309119. [PMC9335188]
growth_records.snippet: Muconic acid is produced from glucose using engineered P. putida at 35 °C for 89 h. The final yield was 0.378 mol/mol glucose (or 0.298 g/g glucose) ... with a production rate of 0.1 g/L/h, corresponding to 50% of the theoretical yield.
growth_records.notes: Secondary data cited within a techno-economic analysis. Reported: muconic acid yield 0.378 mol/mol glucose = 0.298 g/g glucose (50% of theoretical max 0.739 mol/mol = 0.583 g/g); volumetric production rate 0.1 g/L/h; 35 °C, 89 h. Volumetric rate is reported directly (not computed). No biomass reported so specific mmol/gDW/h not derivable. No growth rate reported. Note: elsewhere the paper cites E. coli reaching 36.8 g/L in a 2-L fed-batch reactor and C. glutamicum reaching 85 g/L (different hosts, not the modeled P. putida process; not curated as separate records because their yields/rates are not individually stated).

growth_records.medium: Minimal salt medium (MSM) with glucose; production feed contained 3-fluorobenzoate

up.method: substrate concentration reported; no specific uptake rate derivable

sec.method: reported volumetric productivity q_2-FMA at continuous steady state (biofilm)

growth_records.cite: Hernández-Sancho et al. Cascading recombinase memory switch for programmable and stable gene expression in Pseudomonas putida. Nucleic Acids Res (2026). DOI:10.1093/nar/gkag589. [PMC13253031]
growth_records.snippet: the highest volumetric productivity for 2-FMA (q2-FMA) = 83 +/- 2 umol l-1 h-1 ... During the first 3 h, the dilution rate (D) was D = 0.9 h-1 ... The dilution rate was then reduced to D = 0.4 h-1.
growth_records.notes: Continuous biofilm biotransformation. Two dilution phases: D=0.9 h^-1 for 3 h (wash out planktonic cells; OD600 fell from ~3 to ~0.3) then D=0.4 h^-1 for production (recorded). Reported volumetric productivity q_2-FMA = 83 +/- 2 umol/L/h (2-FMA secretion). Because product-forming biomass is a retained biofilm, D does NOT equal specific growth rate mu, so no mu recorded; biomass in gDW not reported so specific (mmol/gDW/h) rates not derivable. 3-FBz feed 10 mM accumulated to ~8 mM, 2-FMA peaked ~0.5 mM. Bioreactor temperature not restated in text (P. putida cultivated at 30 C elsewhere in the study); left null to avoid inventing. Batch-phase specific growth rates (mu) shown only graphically (Fig 3F) with no absolute numeric value in text (switch strain within 12% of control).

growth_records.strain: KT2440-derived control/parental strain TEAM-2595 (integrated isoprenol pathway)
growth_records.cite: Menasalvas et al. Biosensor-driven strain engineering reveals key cellular processes for maximizing isoprenol production in Pseudomonas putida. Science Advances (2025). DOI:10.1126/sciadv.ady2677. [PMC12551699]
growth_records.snippet: TEAM-2595 grown in 5-ml culture tubes produced up to 200 mg/liter of isoprenol, whereas the same strain grown in the deep-well plate format only produced ~25 mg/liter (Fig. 4D), suggesting that cell physiology was favorable for isoprenol pr
growth_records.notes: Engineered/synthetic-biology titer paper; the ONLY experimental product measurements are cumulative isoprenol titers (mg/L), NOT specific secretion rates. No growth rate, doubling time, glucose uptake rate, yield, or MFA flux is reported numerically anywhere in text (grep of full body: 0 hits for mmol, gDW, mmol/gDW/h, OD600, doubling; growth rate discussed only qualitatively). Cannot compute qP in mmol/gDW/h because biomass and culture time-course biomass are not given. Isoprenol MW ~86.13 g/mol if unit conversion later needed (200 mg/L = 2.32 mmol/L). Control/parental strain TEAM-2595 carries the integrated isoprenol pathway. Two culture formats reported: 5-mL tube (up to 200 mg/L) and 24-deep-well plate (~25 mg/L). 48-h titers corrected for ~43% evaporative loss via formula ([isoprenol_24h]x0.43)+[isoprenol_48h]. Medium is authors' 'NREL M9'/'Modified M9'.

growth_records.snippet: In the quantitative assessment of the MGR on glucose, regression analysis was applied to calculate the specific growth rate of P. putida B6-2 on glucose, and the glucose maximum uptake rate of the model wild_type_B6-2 was referenced from P.
growth_records.notes: GEM (gapseq) genome-streamlining paper. Only quantitative rate present: glucose maximum uptake rate = 6.1 mmol/g/h (i.e., 6.1 mmol gDW^-1 h^-1) applied as the FBA uptake upper bound for model wild_type_B6-2 -- this value is CITED from P. putida KT2440 (reference 54), NOT experimentally measured for strain B6-2 here, so is_experimental=false. An in vitro specific growth rate (MGR) of P. putida B6-2 on glucose WAS experimentally measured (regression of a growth curve on MSM + 2 g/L glucose) and used to validate the GEM: text states 'the difference between the in silico prediction and the in vitro measurements was lower than 20%' (Fig. 2b), but the numeric measured growth-rate value is only in the Fig. 2b image / Tables S4-S6 and is NOT in the extractable text, so growth_rate_per_h left null (no invented number). GEM-predicted MGR improvements of streamlined strain BGR4 over B6-2 were 1.15%-4.46% (predicted, not measured). Growth on 10 g/L glycerol and 2 mM phenol reported only qualitatively (BGR4 showed ~12 h shorter lag and 0.7-fold higher max OD600 on glycerol; ~24 h shorter lag and 1.1-fold higher max phenol degradation rate with dmpKLMNOP), with no numeric mu or gDW-normalized rates. Temperature/pH/rpm not stated in extractable text. Other three papers in this batch report no prokaryote physiological rates.

growth_records.snippet: Because growth on ribose of wild-type P. putida was slow (μ ∼ 0.06 h−1), we confirmed the mutual essentiality of G6PDH-A and G6PDH-B by growing the ΔzwfAB strain on M9 minimal medium agar plates containing ribose for 180 h
growth_records.notes: Experimental specific growth rate stated verbatim in text: μ ≈ 0.06 h−1 for WT KT2440 on ribose in M9 (approximate, prefixed '∼'). doubling_time = ln2/μ = 0.6931/0.06 = 11.55 h (calculated). Carbon: 'M9 minimal medium ... supplemented with 20 mM citrate, 20 mM glucose, 24 mM ribose, 20 mM fructose, 40 mM glycerol, or 20 mM gluconate ... 120 mM total carbon'. Base M9 salt composition not given in text (cited to reference 24). Growth rates 'determined by fitting the temporal changes in OD600 to the exponential growth model' (n=3). No experimental substrate-uptake or product-secretion rates and no own 13C-MFA reported in this paper (paper explicitly contrasts steady-state 13C-MFA with its own batch growth); enzyme kcat/Km values reported are protein-level, not organism physiology.

mfa.reaction_or_metabolite: net glycolytic flux through the GAPDH node during growth on glucose
mfa.note: CITED from Kukurugya et al. 2019 J Biol Chem 294:8464 (ref 14), used as an experimentally-constrained MFA target for the GAPDH-node model (Fig. 6A); NOT measured in this paper. Glycolytic carbon on glucose is channeled primarily through the Entner-Doudoroff pathway (6PG -> pyruvate + GAP) toward the TCA cycle.

growth_records.method: reported (measured; numeric value only in figures/SI, not in extractable text)
growth_records.snippet: Growth rates on 100 mM C glucose as the sole carbon source... our model must produce a net glycolytic flux at 66% of glucose uptake rate (14)... During growth on glucose, high glycolytic carbon flux was channeled primarily through the Entne
growth_records.notes: Glucose (glycolytic substrate) condition for wild-type P. putida KT2440. This is a 13C-kinetic-isotope-tracing + quantitative-metabolomics + proteomics study of the GAPDH node, NOT a rate-quantification MFA paper. NO numeric measured uptake/secretion (mmol/gDW/h) or numeric mu is present in the extractable main text (grep: mmol=0, gDW=0, h^-1 absent). Growth rates were measured but appear only in figures/SI (Fig. 3C, SI Figs. S4/S6). Intracellular metabolites were quantified as umol gCDW^-1 (relative log2 fold-change GLC vs FER), not exchange fluxes. The only numeric flux (66% of J_G glycolytic) is CITED from ref 14 (Kukurugya 2019) as a model constraint, not measured here. Medium recipe, temperature and pH are in SI Appendix only, hence medium_composition_stated=false. No derived rate computed (no inputs available).

growth_records.method: reported (measured; numeric value only in figures/SI)
growth_records.snippet: Growth rates on 100 mM C ferulate as the sole carbon source. NG: no growth... a net gluconeogenic flux at 15% of the ferulate uptake flux (38) for the respective growth conditions... high carbon flux remained largely in the TCA cycle with m
growth_records.notes: Ferulate (gluconeogenic substrate) condition for wild-type P. putida KT2440. Same caveats as the glucose record: NO numeric measured uptake/secretion or mu in extractable text; growth measured but in figures/SI only. Only numeric flux (15% of J_F gluconeogenic) is CITED from ref 38 as a model constraint, not measured here. Key biological finding: metabolic segregation - after 13C switching, glucose-derived carbons did not enter gluconeogenesis in ferulate-acclimated cells and ferulate-derived carbons did not enter lower glycolysis/TCA in glucose-acclimated cells, controlled by GapA (glycolytic, 22-fold up on glucose) vs GapB (gluconeogenic, unchanged). Medium/temp/pH in SI only -> medium_composition_stated=false. No derived rate computed.

growth_records.notes: Authors' own primary fed-batch experiment (not cited from another paper). Substrate consumption (28.0 g/L glucose, 12.5 g/L xylose) and product (1.9 g/L isoprenyl acetate) are endpoint amounts; NO specific rate in mmol/gDW/h, NO growth rate (mu) or doubling time (run duration and time-resolved biomass only in Fig. 8, not numeric in text), NO 13C-MFA. Arithmetic check: titer / total-fed-sugar = 1.9/(28.0+12.5)=1.9/40.5=0.047 g/g, which does NOT equal the stated 0.067 g/g -> authors' 0.067 basis appears to be sugar actually consumed (or a productive-phase basis) rather than total fed; value 0.067 reported verbatim, discrepancy flagged, NOT recomputed. Biomass only as max OD600=13.2 (no gDW), so qS/qP not derivable. Aerobic, pH 6.5, 30 C.

No acetate/soluble byproducts (1H-NMR).

growth_records.snippet: an increased glucose uptake rate of 1.2 mmol/(gCDW h) for pPS05 and 3.2 mmol/(gCDW h) for pSynPro8 ... corresponding to a carbon yield of 40% [CmolRL/CmolGlc], which is approximately 55% of the theoretical yield ... 47 mg/(gCDW h)
growth_records.notes: Table 3 (line 2) attributes physiological parameters of KT2440 pSynPro8 to Wittgens (2013) thesis; glucose uptake 3.2 mmol/gCDW/h stated in-text (sec 3.3) as observed in this study. Discrepancy in paper: sec 3.3 gives qGlc=3.2 for pSynPro8 while flux-estimation section (sec 3.5) gives the best strain qGlc=1.3 mmol/gCDW/h with RL rate 0.1 mmol/gCDW/h — both recorded. Specific RL rate 47 mg/(gCDW h)=0.047 g/(gCDW h). Table 3: CDW 3.1 gCDW/L, max titer 3.2 gRL/L, carbon yield 0.40 Cmol/Cmol (55% theor.), process time 22 h, space-time yield 146.4 mgRL/(L h). Growth limited after ~5 h (complex-medium diauxie); no clean mu reported for this strain.

product_yields.note: 2.75 g/L VA from 63.3 g glucose + 34.2 g xylose + 42.9 g arabinose (per L) = 140.4 g/L total sugar consumed; mass yield ~= 2.75/140.4 = 0.0196 g VA/g sugar (derived, cumulative)

growth_records.cite: Wu, Peihan et al. Engineering mixed sugar metabolic channels in Pseudomonas putida to produce vanillic acid. Synthetic and Systems Biotechnology (2025). DOI:10.1016/j.synbio.2025.10.002. [PMC12552152]
growth_records.snippet: After 120 h fermentation, the strain VA12 consumed 63.3 g/L of glucose, 34.2 g/L of xylose, and 42.9 g/L of arabinose to produce 2.75 g/L vanillic acid ... a 5.6-fold increase in productivity of the shake flask experiment
growth_records.notes: Fed-batch 6-L bioreactor (4 L working). pH 7.0+/-0.5 (NH4OH), DO 30%, airflow 1 L/min, stirrer 200-900 rpm, aerobic. Temperature not explicitly stated for bioreactor; seed and all shake-flask work at 30 C, assumed 30 C (flagged). Cumulative consumption over 120 h: glucose 63.3, xylose 34.2, arabinose 42.9 g/L (values exceed initial charge because sugars fed twice daily 24-96 h). Derived average volumetric uptake: glucose 63.3/120=0.5275, xylose 34.2/120=0.285, arabinose 42.9/120=0.3575 g/L/h. VA productivity 2.75/120=0.0229 g/L/h. Total sugar consumed 140.4 g/L -> mass yield ~0.0196 g/g. Cannot give mmol/gDW/h: biomass/DCW not reported numerically (growth curve Fig 6B only). 2.75 g/L stated as best reported yield for glucose-xylose-arabinose to vanillic acid in P. putida.

sec.method: stoichiometric: benzoate degradation via beta-ketoadipate (ortho-cleavage) yields equimolar CO2, succinate and acetyl-CoA (net-flux description; numeric value not tabulated in text)

mfa.note: entry flux into beta-ketoadipate ortho-cleavage pathway at steady state, D=0.2 h-1

net-flux map (Fig 2) estimated with FiatFlux NETTO; degradation gives equimolar CO2, succinate and acetyl-CoA; prominent glyoxylate-cycle/malate-dehydrogenase flux reported; individual numeric fluxes only shown in figure, not in text

growth_records.cite: Sudarsan et al. Dynamics of benzoate metabolism in Pseudomonas putida KT2440. Metabolic Engineering Communications (2016). DOI:10.1016/j.meteno.2016.03.005. [PMC5779716]
growth_records.snippet: Benzoate uptake in P. putida is driven by an active transport system, which has a specific uptake rate of 3.4 mmol benzoate gCDW-1 h-1 (or 0.5 g benzoate gCDW-1 h-1). Benzoate degradation via the beta-ketoadipate (ortho-cleavage) pathway le
growth_records.notes: Benzoate-limited chemostat steady state, D=0.2 h-1 so mu = D = 0.2 h-1 (steady-state assumption; text: 'mu is the specific growth rate or dilution rate during continuous cultivation'). doubling_time = ln2/mu = 0.693/0.2 = 3.47 h (calculated). Specific benzoate uptake 3.4 mmol/gCDW/h (=0.5 g/gCDW/h) computed by the authors from the steady-state mass balance qBZT = D(Cfeed - Cex)/Cx (Eq.1); is_experimental true. Reactor T=30 C, pH controlled 7.0 (medium pH 7.1), aerated at 2 L/min; biomass ~5 gCDW/L (OD450 ~30). Net central-carbon fluxes estimated with FiatFlux NETTO from GEM stoichiometry (Nogales 2008) + measured physiological rates + E. coli biomass precursor demands (P/O=1); numeric flux values are only rendered in the Fig 2 flux map, so only the benzoate entry flux is captured numerically here. CO2/succinate/acetyl-CoA marked non-experimental (stoichiometric statement, no number in text).

growth_records.notes: CITED FROM ANOTHER PAPER: Batianis et al. (2022). Table 2 literature-comparison row. P. putida batch shake flask, M9, ~12 g/L glucose over 24 h. 2-KIV 0.81 g/L, Yx/s ~0.07, Yp/s 0.40, Yp/x ~0.95 g/g. Several values marked approximate in table.

growth_records.cite: Jiang, Xuan Jade et al. Fed-batch production of MCL-PHA with elevated 3-hydroxynonanoate content. AMB Express (2013). DOI:10.1186/2191-0855-3-50. [PMC3846598]
growth_records.snippet: Co-feeding nonanoic acid (NA), glucose (G), and acrylic acid (AA) at a mass ratio of 1.25:1:0.01 and a specific growth rate of 0.25 h-1 produced 34 g L-1 dry biomass containing a maximum of 56% PHA... the PHA contained 81 mol% HN which incr
growth_records.notes: THIS STUDY, fed-batch fermentation #1 (Table 1 row 3). Imposed mu=0.25 h-1 via exponential feeding (setpoint, growth-limiting carbon). Doubling time td=ln2/mu=0.693/0.25=2.77 h. Yx/c=0.62 g biomass/g total carbon (experimental, slope of trend line). YPHA/NA=0.68 g PHA/g nonanoic acid (experimental slope). Specific uptake qS computed as mu/Yx/c=0.25/0.62=0.403 g/gDW/h; split into NA and glucose using stated mass fractions fNA=0.56, fG=0.44 (from 1.25:1 NA:G ratio): qNA=0.403x0.56=0.226, qG=0.403x0.44=0.177 g/gDW/h (derived, not directly measured; MW nonanoic acid 158.24, glucose 180.16 for optional mmol conversion). PHA productivity 1.2 g/L/h is cumulative VOLUMETRIC (PHA g/L / total time), not specific. Fermentation ended early due to uncontrollable foaming ~15.8 h. Biomass measured gravimetrically; glucose colorimetric; nonanoic acid GC-FID after methylation.

growth_records.medium: Defined mineral salts medium with nonanoic acid + glucose + acrylic acid co-feed
growth_records.cite: Jiang, Xuan Jade et al. Fed-batch production of MCL-PHA with elevated 3-hydroxynonanoate content. AMB Express (2013). DOI:10.1186/2191-0855-3-50. [PMC3846598]
growth_records.snippet: the culture conditions were kept the same as above except that the amount of acrylic acid was increased by a factor of five... only 17 g L-1 final biomass was produced... the PHA content increased from 56 to 64% with about 90 mol% HN
growth_records.notes: THIS STUDY, fed-batch fermentation #2 (Table 1 row 4). Imposed mu=0.25 h-1. td=0.693/0.25=2.77 h. Yx/c=0.53 g/g (lower due to higher acrylic-acid inhibition), YPHA/NA=0.68 g/g (both experimental slopes). qS=mu/Yx/c=0.25/0.53=0.472 g/gDW/h; NA/glucose split via fNA=0.56/fG=0.44: qNA=0.264, qG=0.208 g/gDW/h (derived). Higher acrylic acid caused earlier nonanoic/acrylic acid accumulation (12.4 h) and foaming, limiting final biomass to 17.1 g/L. PHA productivity 0.8 g/L/h is volumetric cumulative.

growth_records.medium: Defined mineral salts medium with nonanoic acid + glucose + acrylic acid co-feed
growth_records.cite: Jiang, Xuan Jade et al. Fed-batch production of MCL-PHA with elevated 3-hydroxynonanoate content. AMB Express (2013). DOI:10.1186/2191-0855-3-50. [PMC3846598]
growth_records.snippet: a final biomass concentration of 71.4 g L-1 was achieved. The PHA content increased in two steps, from 0 to 59.8%... a second increase from 58.1 to 75.5%... the best MCL-PHA productivity (1.8 g L-1 h-1) and yield of PHA from NA (0.78 g g-1)
growth_records.notes: THIS STUDY, best fed-batch fermentation #3 (Table 1 row 5). Imposed mu=0.15 h-1 (exponential phase, first 23.3 h) then switched to constant feed 8 g/L/h. td=ln2/mu=0.693/0.15=4.62 h. Yx/c=0.62, YPHA/NA=0.78 g/g (highest; experimental slopes). qS during exponential phase=mu/Yx/c=0.15/0.62=0.242 g/gDW/h; NA/glucose split fNA=0.56/fG=0.44: qNA=0.135, qG=0.107 g/gDW/h (derived, applies to exponential phase only; constant-feed phase not at fixed mu). Lower growth rate avoided toxic NA/acrylic-acid accumulation, giving high cell density 71.4 g/L and 75.5% PHA. Note DO=0 from 29.6 h onward (late O2 limitation). PHA productivity 1.8 g/L/h volumetric cumulative (best in study).

growth_records.snippet: By the end of the experiment, 3-FBz had been converted into 2-FMA with Y P/S = 0.41 mol mol-1, as well as a residual amount of 3-FC. Both 2-FMA and 3-FC represented 55% (mol mol-1) of the 3-FBz consumed - the remainder was defluorinated and
growth_records.notes: Wild-type KT2440 bioconversion of 10 mM 3-FBz with 30 mM glucose. Product yield Y_P/S(2-FMA)=0.41 mol/mol; 2-FMA+3-FC together=0.55 mol/mol of 3-FBz consumed; remainder defluorinated to biomass. Shaken-flask, 30C, pH 7.0, 250 rpm, 24 h. No numeric qS/qP given for wild-type (only for engineered strains).

up.method: HPLC; 10 mM completely consumed within 20 h; qS not numerically reported

growth_records.snippet: P. putida completely consumed 2-FBz within 20 h, with 2-FMA as the only fluorometabolite detectable upon substrate depletion... For both species, 2-FMA amounted to ca. 14% of the 2-FBz consumed (i.e., molar product yield Y P/S = 0.14 mol mo
growth_records.notes: Wild-type KT2440 on 10 mM 2-FBz + 30 mM glucose: complete 2-FBz consumption in 20 h, Y_P/S(2-FMA)=0.14 mol/mol (85% of F lost as fluoride via non-productive 1,2-dioxygenation). Shaken-flask, 30C, pH 7.0.

growth_records.strain: PMP1053 (engineered KT2440: P_tac(BCD10)->benABC, P_14b(BCD10)->catA, P_14b(BCD10)->catA-II; plasmid-free, third generation)

up.method: qS from Eq1 (1/Xbar * dS/dt); mean+/-0.01 SD of 3 biological replicates; Table S5
30 mM co-substrate for growth; rate not reported

sec.method: qP from Eq2 (1/Xbar * dP/dt); mean+/-0.04 SD of 3 biological replicates; Table S5

growth_records.cite: Wirth, Nicolas T. et al. Combinatorial pathway balancing provides biosynthetic access to 2-fluoro-cis,cis-muconate in engineered Pseudomonas putida. Chem Catalysis (2021). DOI:10.1016/j.checat.2021.09.002. [PMC8711041]
growth_records.snippet: The combination of P tac -> benABC, P 14b(BCD10) -> catA, and P 14b(BCD10) -> catA-II in strain PMP1053 enabled complete conversion of 3-FBz (q S = 1.24 +/- 0.01 mmol g CDW-1 h-1) at maximum theoretical yield and highest specific productivi
growth_records.notes: Best-performing engineered strain. Measured qS(3-FBz)=1.24+/-0.01 mmol/gCDW/h, qP(2-FMA)=0.63+/-0.04 mmol/gCDW/h, complete conversion at maximum theoretical yield Y_P/S=0.5 mol/mol. qP ~= 1/2*qS confirms balanced bioconversion (3-FBz->2-FMA stoichiometry 1:0.5 due to non-productive dioxygenation). Rates via Eq1/Eq2 over timeframe of detectable extracellular change; biomass = OD600*0.35 gCDW/L. On benzoate as sole C source PMP1053 mu_max reduced by 50% and biomass yield by 30% vs wild-type (i.e. mu_max ~= 0.73*0.5 = 0.37 h-1, calculated). Shaken-flask, 30C, pH 7.0, 250 rpm.

growth_records.cite: Poblete-Castro, Ignacio et al. Comparison of mcl-Poly(3-hydroxyalkanoates) synthesis by different Pseudomonas putida strains from crude glycerol: citrate accumulates at high titer under PHA-producing conditions. BMC Biotechnology (2014). DOI:10.1186/s12896-014-0110-z. [PMC4299480]
growth_records.snippet: Both strains had an initial lag-phase of 7 h, after that time they started growing to a maximum specific growth rate of 0.22 h-1 ... Citrate has a final yield of 0.6 [g/g] for both the mutant and the wild type P. putida strain when grown on
growth_records.notes: Aerobic nitrogen-limited batch bioreactor, 30 C, pH 7.0. Measured mu_max = 0.22 h-1 (reported, both KT2440 and dphaZ). doubling_time = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.22 = 3.15 h. Citrate is the only organic acid detected (HPLC); byproduct panel screened = citrate, formate, pyruvate, succinate, malate, acetate (only citrate found). Ycitrate/glycerol = 0.6 g/g; final citrate >20 g/L. No per-gDW/h uptake or secretion rate is reported in the paper, so qS/qCitrate cannot be computed without a time-resolved biomass value (figure-only, not tabulated). Glycerol fully consumed from 30 g/L initial. Biomass yield 0.048 g/g is the shake-flask value for KT2440, listed here for context.

up.method: not reported as rate; 30 g/L initial

sec.units: g/L (endpoint titer at 72 h, NOT a rate)

yields.note: biomass yield 0.048 g/g glycerol; PHA 34.5% of CDW

growth_records.method: not reported (growth rate not given for flask; only lag phase ~8 h)
growth_records.snippet: P. putida KT2440 was by far the most efficient PHA-producing organism among all tested strains, amassing 35% of its CDW as PHA, and achieving a biomass yield of 0.048 (g/g) ... CDW 4.23 +/- 0.11 g/L; PHA 1.46 +/- 0.21 g/L
growth_records.notes: Shake-flask batch, 30 g/L raw glycerol, 30 C, 180 rpm, 72 h (Table 2). CDW 4.23 g/L, PHA 34.5% wt = 1.46 g/L. Biomass yield reported 0.048 g/g. Growth rate NOT reported for flask cultures (only bioreactor gave mu=0.22 h-1); doubling time therefore null. PHA and CDW are endpoint titers, not per-biomass rates. Dominant PHA monomer C10 (3-OH-decanoate) 74.6%, C8 16.2%, C12 6.0%, C10:... per Table 2. pH not controlled in flasks.

growth_records.cite: Blunt, Warren et al. Development of High Cell Density Cultivation Strategies for Improved Medium Chain Length Polyhydroxyalkanoate Productivity Using Pseudomonas putida LS46. Bioengineering (Basel) (2019). DOI:10.3390/bioengineering6040089. [PMC6956024]
growth_records.snippet: By 20 h, the residual NH4 concentration reached 700 mg L-1 ... consumed to a final concentration of 49 mg L-1 by 27 h, resulting in an uptake rate of 5.90 +/- 0.25 mg NH4 g Xr-1 h-1. By comparison, the measured uptake rate of NH4 during the
growth_records.notes: PULSE-FEED strategy record (Table 2). mu_avg/Xr growth phase (0-14 h) = 0.35 +/- 0.11 h-1 (reported); doubling_time = ln2/0.35 = 0.693/0.35 = 1.98 h. Storage phase (14-27 h) mu = 0.03 +/- 0.01 h-1 (near-zero, O2-limited PHA storage). Final total biomass Xt = 28.9 +/- 4.0 g/L, PHA content 60.6 +/- 8.2%, Xr = 11.2 g/L, XPHA = 17.7 g/L, volumetric productivity Qv,final = 0.61 +/- 0.12 (Qv,max 0.66 +/- 0.14 g/L/h at 23-27 h). NH4/PO4 uptake rates from pulse-feed experiment (Fig 1C). Derived octanoic acid uptake: qS = mu/Y_Xr/S = 0.35 h-1 / 0.72 g/g = 0.486 g octanoic/gXr/h; octanoic acid MW C8H16O2 = 144.21 g/mol -> 0.486/144.21*1000 = 3.37 mmol/gXr/h (is_experimental false, derived). Y_PHA/S 0.62 and Y_Xr/N 6.1 were carried over/cited from the authors' prior batch study Blunt et al. (ref [27], same group); Y_Xr/S 0.72 also from prior data. Y_Xr/PO4 13.48 measured here (R2=0.91). 'Previous batch results' column in Table 2 (Xt 2.37 g/L, mu 0.29 growth/0.11 storage, Qv 0.08) are cited from Blunt et al. [27], NOT measured in this paper, so not emitted as a separate record.

sec.units: g PHA / g Xr / h (Qs,max, 23-25 h)

yields.Yxs: Y_Xr/S = 0.72 g Xr / g octanoic (from prior work)

growth_records.cite: Blunt, Warren et al. Development of High Cell Density Cultivation Strategies for Improved Medium Chain Length Polyhydroxyalkanoate Productivity Using Pseudomonas putida LS46. Bioengineering (Basel) (2019). DOI:10.3390/bioengineering6040089. [PMC6956024]
growth_records.snippet: the drip feed strategy appeared to favor production of Xr over XPHA, as indicated by the slightly higher [Xt], lower %PHA, lower Y_PHA/S, and a lower rate of PHA synthesis ... during the accumulation phase.
growth_records.notes: CONTINUOUS DRIP-FEED strategy record (Table 2). mu_avg/Xr growth phase (0-14 h) = 0.31 +/- 0.03 h-1 (reported); doubling = ln2/0.31 = 0.693/0.31 = 2.24 h. Storage phase mu = 0.03 +/- 0.01 h-1. Final Xt = 32.4 +/- 0.9 g/L, %PHA 52.9 +/- 2.5, Xr 17.4 g/L, XPHA 15.4 g/L, Qv,final = 0.60 +/- 0.04 g/L/h. Derived octanoic uptake qS = 0.31/0.72 = 0.431 g/gXr/h /144.21*1000 = 2.98 mmol/gXr/h (derived). NH4/PO4 specific uptake rates were reported only for the pulse-feed experiment; text states drip-feed concentration profiles for DO, carbon and NH4 behaved similarly. Y_PHA/S and Y_Xr/S baseline values cited from Blunt et al. [27].

growth_records.cite: Xi Wang et al. Engineering isoprenoids production in metabolically versatile microbial host Pseudomonas putida. Biotechnology for Biofuels and Bioproducts (2022). DOI:10.1186/s13068-022-02235-6. [PMC9743605]
growth_records.method: not_derivable (growth reported only as OD600 time-course in figures; no biomass DW or mu given)
growth_records.snippet: the strain with MK_Mm-PMD_HKQ (JPUB_019977) produced the highest isoprenol at 104 mg/L after 48 h from 2% glucose in a culture tube... ~2.5 g/L of residual glucose was detected after 48 h... small amount of acetate (0.8 g/L) and succinate (
growth_records.notes: Engineered heterologous producer; only VOLUMETRIC rates derivable (no biomass DW reported, so no mmol/gDW/h and no mu). Glucose uptake: feed 20 g/L (2% w/v) - residual 2.5 g/L = 17.5 g/L consumed over 48 h => 17.5/48 = 0.365 g/L/h (=17500/180.16=97.1 mmol/L /48h=2.02 mmol/L/h). Isoprenol secretion: 104 mg/L /48 h = 2.17 mg/L/h (MW 86.13; 104/86.13=1.208 mmol/L /48h=0.0252 mmol/L/h). Acetate: 0.8 g/L at 24 h => 0.8/24=0.033 g/L/h (transient, gone later). Succinate: 0.2 g/L at 24 h => 0.2/24=0.0083 g/L/h. Growth reported only as OD600 curves in Fig 3 (not extractable).

Titer declines after 24 h (native isoprenol re-consumption by P. putida). No biomass DW; no mu.
